questions.title: Reading – Text 1: Helping Our Waterways
questions.content:
Read the following information from posters about protecting waterways.

Poster 1: What you do in your garden ends up in our waterways. Prevent run-off from fertilisers and pesticides. Apply carefully and only when the weather is fine. THE DRAIN IS JUST FOR RAIN

Poster 2: What you do in your garden ends up in our waterways. Leaves and clippings remove oxygen from water, killing aquatic life. Keep garden waste out of the gutter. Compost or mulch it. THE DRAIN IS JUST FOR RAIN

questions.title: Reading – Text 1: Helping Our Waterways
questions.content: What is the main message of both posters?
options: Garden chemicals and waste can harm waterways
correctAnswers: Garden chemicals and waste can harm waterways

questions.title: Reading – Text 1: Helping Our Waterways
questions.content: The phrase 'THE DRAIN IS JUST FOR RAIN' is written in capital letters to —
options: show it is the most important message
correctAnswers: show it is the most important message

questions.title: Reading – Text 1: Helping Our Waterways
questions.content: According to Poster 2, why should you not put leaves in the gutter?
options: They remove oxygen from water and kill aquatic life
They make the water smell nice
correctAnswers: They remove oxygen from water and kill aquatic life

questions.title: Reading – Text 1: Helping Our Waterways
questions.content: The best alternative to putting garden waste in the gutter is to —
options: leave it on the lawn forever

questions.title: Reading – Text 2: How to Play SPUD
questions.content:
SPUD is a fun ball game for five or more players.
You need: a soft rubber ball and a clear outdoor space.
Aim: To be the last player left in the game.
How to play:
1. One player is chosen to be It.
2. It throws the ball high in the air and yells a player's name.
3. That player must catch the ball. Everyone else runs away.
4. It yells 'SPUD!' as soon as It gets the ball. Everyone else has to freeze.
5. Then It takes three giant steps towards another player, and throws the ball at that player’s feet. The other player must not move.
6. If It hits the player, or the player moves, that player gets a letter (starting with S) and becomes It. If It misses, It gets a letter.
7. Letters spell S-P-U-D. The first to spell SPUD is out. Last player left wins.

questions.title: Reading – Text 2: How to Play SPUD
questions.content: What must happen right after It gets the ball back?

questions.title: Reading – Text 2: How to Play SPUD
questions.content: Why do players have to freeze when 'SPUD!' is called?
options: To make the game harder for It
So It can aim and throw at their feet
correctAnswers: So It can aim and throw at their feet

questions.title: Reading – Text 2: How to Play SPUD
questions.content: If a player is hit by the ball and does not move, what happens next?
options: They get a letter and become It
correctAnswers: They get a letter and become It

questions.title: Reading – Text 2: How to Play SPUD
questions.content: The word 'giant' in 'three giant steps' most likely means —

questions.title: Reading – Text 3: The Outsider (short narrative extract)
questions.content:
On an Arctic island long ago, a stranger is approaching a village.

'Papa,' I yell. 'Someone is coming.'

My father looks up from fixing the sled. His face tightens. Strangers are rare here. They usually bring news — or trouble.

The figure wears thick furs and moves slowly across the ice. A dog pulls a small sled behind. As he gets closer, I see his face is covered in frost. He raises a hand in greeting.

Papa steps forward. 'Who are you, traveller? What brings you to our village?'

The stranger pulls down his hood. 'I am Kael from the eastern coast. My people are hungry. The seals have gone. I seek trade… or help.'

Papa looks at me, then back at Kael. 'Come inside. Warm yourself. We will talk.'

questions.title: Reading – Text 3: The Outsider (short narrative extract)
questions.content: Where is the story set?

questions.title: Reading – Text 3: The Outsider (short narrative extract)
questions.content: How does the narrator feel when they first see the stranger?

questions.title: Reading – Text 3: The Outsider (short narrative extract)
questions.content: Why has Kael come to the village?
options: Because his people are hungry and need help or trade
correctAnswers: Because his people are hungry and need help or trade

questions.title: Reading – Text 3: The Outsider (short narrative extract)
questions.content: What does Papa's face tightening most likely show?

questions.title: Reading – Text 3: The Outsider (short narrative extract)
questions.content: What does Papa do to show he is willing to listen?
options: Invites Kael inside to warm up and talk
correctAnswers: Invites Kael inside to warm up and talk
